_legend.purpose: Plain-language one-sentence summary per POV doc. Shown on goal cards as a 'What does this mean?' expansion beneath the main description. Also available for future UI surfaces (Today screen nudges, email body).
_legend.style: Second person. One sentence (two max). No jargon. When jargon is unavoidable (TDEE, 1RM, hypertrophy), define it in the same sentence. Read it out loud before accepting — if it sounds like a textbook, rewrite.
_legend.fallback: Empty string means no summary yet. UI falls back to showing only the main goal description.
_legend._note: Keys starting with _ are ignored by the loader. First-pass copy for templated docs only; uncurated docs can be filled in as they become relevant.

05-supplements: A short stack of cheap, well-studied supplements — creatine, vitamin D, omega-3, magnesium glycinate — at effective doses does more than any multivitamin. Multivitamins underdose the things that matter and duplicate the things you already get from food.
07-skincare-antiaging: A simple routine — cleanser, moisturizer, sunscreen, and a retinoid at night — protects your skin from the sun damage that ages you and keeps your face looking sharper for longer.
08-head-hair-balding: Early, boring action on your hair — the right cut for your face, consistent product, and treatment at the first sign of loss — keeps you looking your best for years longer than waiting will.
09-facial-hair: Pick a beard or stubble style that matches how your facial hair actually grows and the shape of your face, then maintain it. The right choice can add visual maturity and balance to almost any face.
10-grooming: Five minutes a day on the boring stuff — eyebrows, nails, ears, nose, skin — is what most men skip and what separates good presentation from mediocre.
11-teeth-smile: Clean, white, reasonably straight teeth do more for your face than almost any cosmetic procedure. Start with a dental cleaning and decide what's worth fixing from there.
12-style-clothing: How clothes fit matters more than how expensive they are. A cheap shirt that fits well beats a pricey one that doesn't — every time.
16-facial-definition-jawline: A sharper jawline is almost always a result of losing body fat, not a special exercise. Lean out and the structure you already have shows up on its own.
17-environment-lifestyle-design: Your habits are shaped more by your environment than your willpower. Remove friction from the things you want to do and add friction to the things you don't.
18-tanning: A subtle tan makes your skin, muscles, and eye whites all read sharper. Get it slowly with short outdoor sessions and sunscreen on your face.
19-strength-training: Lift three times a week, biased toward the upper-body muscles that show — shoulders, chest, back, arms — with a leg movement and hinge as foundation. Progressive overload over months beats any fancier program.
20-diet-macros: TDEE (total daily energy expenditure) is how many calories you burn in a day. Eat a bit under it to lose fat, a bit over to build muscle, with enough protein either way to protect your muscle.
21-protein-creatine: Aim for about your body weight in grams of protein per day, spread across three to five meals. Creatine monohydrate (five grams daily) is the one supplement that reliably helps — after the protein is handled.
23-cardio: Easy-pace cardio — like a brisk walk or a light jog — two to four times a week supports fat loss and long-term health without interfering with your lifting.
25-acne: Acne is four things at once: oil, bacteria, clogged pores, and inflammation. A simple routine that treats all four (wash, benzoyl peroxide or salicylic acid, retinoid) beats any spot treatment.
27-hair-loss-treatments: Minoxidil and finasteride are the two evidence-backed treatments for male hair loss. They work best when you start them early, before the loss is obvious.
29-body-hair-methods: Trimming or removing body hair where it makes your physique read cleaner — chest, back, abdomen — is a quick win. Pick the method that fits your pain tolerance and schedule.
30-appetite-control: Hunger isn't a willpower problem — it's about what you eat, when, and where. Protein-forward meals, complex carbs, fiber, and a regular eating schedule beat white-knuckling through cravings.
31-calorie-macro-framework: Figure out roughly how many calories you burn in a day, subtract 300–500 to lose fat or add 200–300 to build muscle, then hit about a gram of protein per pound of bodyweight. Two to three weeks of tracking tells you if the math was right for you — adjust from there.
32-skin-texture-scarring: Smoother skin starts with a retinoid and consistent exfoliation. Stubborn texture or scarring usually needs a professional treatment to really move — it's worth the visit.
33-niche-enhancements: The finishing layer — clean nails, a well-chosen watch, glasses that actually suit your face, one subtle accessory at most. Small things that stack into a more intentional presentation, but only worth investing in once body composition, skin, hair, and grooming are already solid.
34-recovery-tools-polish: Sauna, cold water on the face, facial massage, and consistent sodium and hydration. Polish tools that sharpen day-to-day appearance on top of good sleep, diet, and training — they don't replace the basics, but they add real finish once the basics are in place.
35-gut-health-fiber: About thirty grams of fiber a day from whole foods plus some fermented foods — yogurt, kimchi, kefir — does more for digestion, skin, and inflammation than any gut-health supplement.
42-sleep: Same sleep and wake times most nights, dark room, no screens in the last hour, seven to nine hours. The single most underrated thing you can do for your appearance, energy, and mood.
45-meal-plans: Three to five meals you actually enjoy, repeated most days, hitting your protein and calorie targets. Boring repetition is what consistency looks like — variety is overrated.
46-mobility: Ten minutes a day on hips, upper back, and ankles keeps you training pain-free now and standing upright as you age.
47-eye-health: The skin around your eyes shows inflammation, dehydration, and bad sleep before anywhere else. Fix those first, then add an eye-area moisturizer and SPF.
48-skin-tone-guidance: Certain colors flatter your skin tone and others wash you out. Wearing the right ones next to your face quietly upgrades how you read in any setting.
50-posture: Standing and sitting upright isn't just a mom lecture — it adds visible height, sharpens your jawline, and signals confidence before you say a word.
54-when-to-stop: Real self-improvement includes knowing when to stop. Checking in weekly on whether the work is still serving you — not just whether the numbers are moving — is part of the practice.
55-limits-self-improvement: Every variable has a ceiling and diminishing returns. Part of getting better is recognizing when you've hit yours on something and moving your attention somewhere else.
56-identity-beyond-appearance: Your worth is not in your appearance. Investing in skills, relationships, and work is what makes the physical stuff sustainable — and what holds up on the days your face isn't cooperating.
